questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Because evolution is a theory, that means:
options: It is a guess or a hunch.
It has very little evidence to support it.
Scientists may or may not believe in it.
It has never been observed.
None of the above
questions.expectedAnswer: None of the above

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Why is understanding evolution important?
options: Understanding evolution can help us understand biodiversity issues associated with deforestation and global warming.
Understanding evolution can help us understand the evolution of antibiotic resistance and cancer.
Understanding evolution can help us understand our own genetic makeup and how it affects our lives.
All of the above
questions.expectedAnswer: All of the above

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about evolution is true?
options: Once biologists find all the missing links, they will be able to understand evolution.
Evolution is a process that leads to more and more complex organisms.
Evolution is entirely random.
Some forms of life are higher on the ladder than other forms of life.
None of the above is a true statement.
questions.expectedAnswer: None of the above is a true statement.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Biological evolution
questions.expectedAnswer: Occurs when genetic material from different strains gets mixed into new combinations within a single individual

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Genetic drift
questions.expectedAnswer: A mechanism that can lead to evolution, whereby differential survival and reproduction of individuals cause some of them to survive and reproduce more effectively than others

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Homologous traits
questions.expectedAnswer: Any change in the inherited traits of a population that occurs from one generation to the next (i.e., over a time period longer than the lifetime of an individual in the population)

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Lineage
questions.expectedAnswer: Any change to the genomic sequence of an organism

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Mutation
questions.expectedAnswer: Characteristics that are similar in two or more species because they are inherited from a common ancestor

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Natural selection
questions.expectedAnswer: Evolution arising from random changes in the genetic composition of a population from one generation to the next

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Phenotypes
questions.expectedAnswer: A chain of ancestors and their descendants. It may be the successive generations of organisms in a single population, the members of an entire species during an interval of geological time, or a group of related species descending from a common ancestor

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Phylogeny
questions.expectedAnswer: A visual representation of the evolutionary history of populations, genes, or species

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Synapomorphy
questions.expectedAnswer: A derived form of a trait that is shared by a group of related species (i.e., one that evolved in the immediate common ancestor of the group and was inherited by all its descendants)

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Viral reassortment
questions.expectedAnswer: Measurable aspects of organisms, such as morphology (structure), physiology, and behavior. Genes interact with other genes and with the environment during the development of the phenotype

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: Why did Georges Cuvier reject Lamarck's idea that species were not fixed?
options: Because he and Lamarck were competitors at the National Museum of Natural History in Paris
Because Lamarck did not believe in extinction
Because his experience indicated that huge gaps existed among major groups of animal fossils with no intermediate fossils
Because his experience with fossils indicated that all species were created at the same time
Because he believed the Earth was not old enough for species to change f. Because he was a creationist (although he thought new creations occurred after catastrophic extinctions)
questions.expectedAnswer: Because his experience indicated that huge gaps existed among major groups of animal fossils with no intermediate fossils

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: How do you define a scientific theory?
options: A guess based on a few facts that scientists try to prove as correct
An educated guess based on some experience that allows scientists to test evidence
A set of laws that define the natural world
A testable set of mechanisms or principles that explain a major aspect of the natural world
A belief that scientists try to prove as fact
questions.expectedAnswer: A testable set of mechanisms or principles that explain a major aspect of the natural world

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: Why did Thomas Malthus' arguments in *An Essay on the Principle of Population* inspire Darwin and Wallace's concept of natural selection?
options: Because Darwin and Wallace realized that poor people could not get enough food no matter how hard they tried
Because Darwin and Wallace believed that populations get what they need, so even humans would not live up to their full reproductive potential
Because Darwin and Wallace needed some support for their ideas about human populations and social engineering
Because Darwin and Wallace realized that one generation of any organism can produce far more individuals than can actually survive and reproduce in the next
questions.expectedAnswer: Because Darwin and Wallace realized that one generation of any organism can produce far more individuals than can actually survive and reproduce in the next

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: Is natural selection the only mechanism that can lead to biological evolution?
options: Yes, because natural selection operates on the needs of organisms in their environment
Yes, because Charles Darwin invented natural selection as part of his theory of evolution
No, because evolution is entirely random
No, because evolution can occur through other mechanisms such as genetic drift
questions.expectedAnswer: No, because evolution can occur through other mechanisms such as genetic drift

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: How would you define biological evolution?
options: A gradual process in which something changes into a different and usually more complex or better form
Any change in the frequency of heritable traits within a population from one generation to the next
A process of slow, progressive change
Any kind of change over time
A theory that humans have their origin in other types of animals, such as apes, and that the distinguishable differences between humans and apes are due to modifications in successive generations
questions.expectedAnswer: Any change in the frequency of heritable traits within a population from one generation to the next

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Why are mutations important in evolution?
options: Because mutations are always deleterious, and organisms with these deleterious mutations do not survive and reproduce
Because mutations only occur in viruses
Because mutations are random, and evolution is a random process
Because mutations create the variation among individuals on which other mechanisms of evolution can act
questions.expectedAnswer: Because mutations create the variation among individuals on which other mechanisms of evolution can act

Because he used a model to generate a prediction for the age of the Earth

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: How confident are scientists about the discoveries related to the earliest signs of life on Earth?
options: Very confident. Two scientists found organic carbon produced by photosynthetic bacteria, so now all scientists accept the 3.7-billion-year-old carbon as the earliest sign of life.
Fairly confident. Stromatolite formation is a process observable today, and scientists have discovered stromatolite fossils that are 3.45 billion year old.
Note very confident. Scientists all have different opinions about the oldest signs of life on Earth.
Not confident. Scientists constantly have to test new techniques, so they can't be sure until they find an identifiable fossil.
It's all just a guess.
questions.expectedAnswer: Fairly confident. Stromatolite formation is a process observable today, and scientists have discovered stromatolite fossils that are 3.45 billion year old.

Living bacteria can offer clues about how the first multicellular animals evolved.
None of the above is a true statement.

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: Did all the different organisms classified as Ediacaran fauna appear in the fossil record at the same time?
options: No. The fossil record indicates that some organisms appeared as early as 575 million years ago, but other organisms did not appear until 20 million years later.
Yes. The fossil record indicates that most organisms in the Ediacaran fauna coexisted, and radiometric dating is not accurate enough to distinguish when different organisms appeared.
Maybe. The fossil record is incomplete, and not enough is known about the Avalon Assemblage to state that the Ediacaran fauna didn't appear at the same time.

Yes. Even though the fossil record indicates that some organisms existed for long periods, and many organisms existed for relatively short periods, scientists cannot know whether these organisms appeared at the same time or not.
No. Scientists are unable to determine the relationships of Ediacaran fauna because they are so diverse.
questions.expectedAnswer: No. The fossil record indicates that some organisms appeared as early as 575 million years ago, but other organisms did not appear until 20 million years later.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: What was Georges Cuvier's contribution to evolutionary theory?
options: He strongly objected to Jean-Baptiste Lamarck's ideas about life evolving from simple to complex, drumming Lamarck out of the scientific establishment.
He invented paleontology.
He recognized that some fossils were both similar to and distinct from living species, and many fossil animals no longer existed.

He was the first to organize and map strata according to a geological history. Incorrect. William Smith discovered that layers of rocks contained distinctive groups of fossils, and he was able to organize strata into a geological history as a result. Cuvier used the system developed by Smith to map strata in other parts of the world (see Section 2.2).
He rejected the idea that species evolved, instead believing that life's history was a series of appearances and extinctions of species.
questions.expectedAnswer: He recognized that some fossils were both similar to and distinct from living species, and many fossil animals no longer existed.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: How does homology relate to the theory of evolution?
options: Homology refers to traits that only superficially look alike, like bat wings and human arms; the theory of evolution cannot explain these similarities.
Homology refers to traits that look alike but have entirely different origins, like the fins of dolphins and of fish; the theory of evolution cannot explain the origins.

Homology refers to traits that are structurally similar in different organisms, like bat wings and human arms, because they each were inherited from a shared common ancestor with those traits; the theory of evolution provides a mechanism for those observations.
Homology refers to traits that have converged on a shared form; the theory of evolution provides a mechanism for those observations.
questions.expectedAnswer: Homology refers to traits that are structurally similar in different organisms, like bat wings and human arms, because they each were inherited from a shared common ancestor with those traits; the theory of evolution provides a mechanism for those observations.

Accurate because radiometric dating can determine estimates for the ages of rocks and fossils, often with relatively small margins of error
Not very accurate because scientists use probabilities to determine decay rates for each isotope they use in radiometric dating

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: Can a mutation that occurs within a cell during mitosis in a diploid cell be passed on to offspring?

The phylogeny on the left shows that your cousins must be more closely related to each other than you and your sister because trout and goldfish are more closely related to each other than frogs and humans.

questions.expectedAnswer: The phylogeny on the left shows that you and your sister are more closely related to each other than you are to your cousins, just as humans and frogs are more closely related to each other than they are to goldfish or trout.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: What is the difference between polyphenic traits and polygenic traits?
options: Polyphenic traits are the different traits that arise because different alleles lead to different phenotypes; polygenic traits are traits influenced by many genes leading to a continuous distribution of phenotypes over a given range.

Polyphenic traits are the multiple, discrete phenotypes that can arise from different alleles within a population; polygenic traits vary continuously within a population because of heritable variation.

Polyphenic traits are the multiple, discrete phenotypes that can arise from a single genotype depending on environmental circumstances; polygenic traits are traits influenced by many genes leading to a continuous distribution of phenotypic variation over a given range.

Polyphenic traits are traits that result when natural selection favors rare genotypes leading to multiple phenotypes within a population; polygenic traits are traits that arise because a single gene affects the expression of many different phenotypic traits.
questions.expectedAnswer: Polyphenic traits are the multiple, discrete phenotypes that can arise from a single genotype depending on environmental circumstances; polygenic traits are traits influenced by many genes leading to a continuous distribution of phenotypic variation over a given range.

questions.heading: what-and-why

Organisms can inherit characters that were acquired during their parents' lifetime.
Both a and b
Both b and c
questions.expectedAnswer: Both a and b

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Can genes that respond to environmental stimuli be passed on to offspring?
options: Yes. Individuals that learn how to respond to the environment can pass that information to their offspring.
Yes. Individuals can inherit the mechanisms that respond to the environment.

No. The only "environmental" influences on gene expression that can be inherited come from other gene products, such as hormones, transcription factors, and cis- and trans-acting elements. Any effects of external environmental factors are not encoded by the genome, so they cannot be heritable.
No. Environmental factors, such as the amount of food available to an individual or the temperature an egg is exposed to, are not heritable.
questions.expectedAnswer: Yes. Individuals can inherit the mechanisms that respond to the environment.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Broad sense heritability (H^{s})
questions.expectedAnswer: The proportion of the total phenotypic variance of a trait that is attributable to genetic variance, where genetic variance is represented in its entirety as a single value (i.e., genetic variance is not broken down into different components).

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Narrow sense heritability (h^{s})
questions.expectedAnswer: The proportion of the total phenotypic variance of a trait attributable to the additive effects of alleles (the additive genetic variance). This is the component of variance that causes offspring to resemble their parents, and it causes populations to evolve predictably in response to selection.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Quantitative genetics
questions.expectedAnswer: The pattern of phenotypic expression of a single genotype across a range of environments. In a sense, reaction norms depict how development maps the genotype into the phenotype as a function of the environment.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Quantitative trait loci (QTLs)
questions.expectedAnswer: A statistical measure of the dispersion of trait values about their mean.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Reaction norm
questions.expectedAnswer: This process involves of scanning through the genomes of many different individuals, some with, and others without, a focal trait of interest, to search for markers associated with expression of the trait.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Selection differential (S)
questions.expectedAnswer: Stretches of DNA that are correlated with variation in a phenotypic trait. These regions contain genes, or are linked to genes, that contribute to population differences in a phenotype.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Variance
questions.expectedAnswer: The study of continuous phenotypic traits and their underlying evolutionary mechanisms.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Aposematism**
questions.expectedAnswer: The transfer of alleles from one population to another. It occurs when organisms or their gametes move from one location to another.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Artificial selection**
questions.expectedAnswer: The situation in which strong selection can “sweep” a favorable allele to fixation within a population so fast that there is little opportunity for recombination. In the absence of recombination, alleles in large stretches of DNA flanking the favorable allele will also reach high frequency.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Ecological character displacement**
questions.expectedAnswer: Structures constructed by organisms that can influence their performance or success. Although they are not part of the organism itself, their properties nevertheless reflect the genotype of each individual. Animal examples include the nests constructed by birds and the galls of flies.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Extended phenotypes**
questions.expectedAnswer: Similar to natural selection, except that it results from human activity. When breeders nonrandomly choose individuals with economically favorable traits to use as breeding stock, they impose strong artificial selection on those traits.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Gene flow**
questions.expectedAnswer: The physical proximity of alleles at different loci. Genetic loci that are physically close to one another on the same chromosome are less likely to be separated by recombination during meiosis. Thus they are said to be genetically linked.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Genetic linkage**
questions.expectedAnswer: An antipredator strategy used by a potential prey item to signal danger or a lack of palatability. The most commonly known form is warning coloration, in which the bright coloration of prey that are potentially dangerous can act as a deterrent to potential predators.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: **Selective sweep**
questions.expectedAnswer: Evolution driven by competition between species for a shared resource (e.g., food). Traits evolve in opposing directions, minimizing overlap between the species.

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: Which is NOT one of the three conditions necessary for evolution by natural selection to occur? a. Individuals must differ in the characteristics of a trait. b. The differences among individuals in a trait must be at least partially heritable. c. Some individuals survive and reproduce more successfully than others because of differences in a trait. d. The more an individual needs a trait, the more quickly it will adapt to its environment. e. All are necessary for natural selection to occur.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Bootstrapping
questions.expectedAnswer: The branched genealogical lineage of homologous alleles that traces their evolution back to an ancestral allele

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Coalescence
questions.expectedAnswer: A distance method for reconstructing phylogenies. This method identifies the tree topology with the shortest possible branch lengths given the data.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Distance-matrix methods
questions.expectedAnswer: Procedures for constructing phylogenetic trees by clustering taxa based on the proximity (or distance) between their DNA or protein sequences. These methods place closely related sequences under the same internal branch, and they estimate branch lengths from the observed distances between sequences.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Gene tree
questions.expectedAnswer: The case when the history of a gene differs from the history of the species carrying the gene.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Genomics
questions.expectedAnswer: The study of the structure and function of genomes, including mapping genes and DNA sequencing. The discipline unites molecular and cell biology, classical genetics, and computational science.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Incomplete lineage sorting
questions.expectedAnswer: The process by which, looking back through time, the genealogy of any pair of homologous alleles merges in a common ancestor.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Maximum likelihood and Bayesian methods
questions.expectedAnswer: A method used to determine time based on base-pair substitutions. They use the rates of molecular change to deduce the divergence time between two lineages in a phylogeny, for example. They work best when they can be “calibrated” with other markers of time, such as fossils with known ages and placements.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Maximum parsimony
questions.expectedAnswer: Approaches used to estimate parameter values for a statistical model. They are used in phylogeny reconstruction to find the tree topologies that are most likely, given a precise model for molecular evolution and a particular data set.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Microsatellites
questions.expectedAnswer: A statistical method for estimating the strength of evidence that a particular branch in a phylogeny exists

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Molecular clock
questions.expectedAnswer: Homologous genes separated by a speciation event

options: In artificial selection, breeders manipulate the entire reproductive process of all individuals, so the response to selection is simply the strength of that selection, $R = S$.
In artificial selection, breeders create very strong selection on traits, which can result in a rapid evolutionary response if the heritability of the selected traits is high.
In artificial selection, breeders manipulate narrow sense heritability, $h^2$, by selecting for specific traits.
$R = h^2 \times S$ is the breeder's equation and therefore only applies to artificial selection.
questions.expectedAnswer: In artificial selection, breeders create very strong selection on traits, which can result in a rapid evolutionary response if the heritability of the selected traits is high.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Is phenotypic plasticity heritable?
options: No. Phenotypic plasticity is the capacity of an individual to change in response to the environment, and characteristics acquired during an individual's lifetime are not heritable.
Yes. Phenotypic plasticity is the capacity of an individual to change in response to the environment, and this ability to change in response to need is the very basis of evolution.
No. Phenotypes cannot be directly linked to any heritable component of genes, so phenotypic plasticity cannot be heritable.
Yes. Individuals may vary in their plastic responses to the environment, and those responses can be heritable.
questions.expectedAnswer: Yes. Individuals may vary in their plastic responses to the environment, and those responses can be heritable.

questions.heading: what-and-why

Sexual selection does not really differ from natural selection; both require heritable variation in traits that confer some fitness differences to individuals possessing those traits.
No. Sexual selection does not really differ from natural selection, but the optimum trait value under sexual selection can often be at odds with the optimum trait value under natural selection.
Both c and d

Because significant changes in phenotypes may occur within small isolated populations on the fringes of a species' range, leading to rapid speciation and little fossil evidence.
Because species are defined as substantial morphological shifts within the fossil record.
Macroevolutionary biologists should not expect abrupt breaks in the fossil record, because entire populations transform from one form to another in a process called anagenesis.
Macroevolutionary biologists should not expect abrupt breaks in the fossil record. They should expect to find enough fossils to demonstrate a smooth transition from one species to another over time.
questions.expectedAnswer: Because significant changes in phenotypes may occur within small isolated populations on the fringes of a species' range, leading to rapid speciation and little fossil evidence.

ch15.name: Intimate Partnerships
ch15.subtitle: How Species Adapt to Each Other

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: What is coevolution?
options: When one species needs to change because another species that it depended on changed
When two or more species interact, and each acts as an agent of selection causing evolution of the other
Evolutionary changes that are shared among two or more species, such as wings
Evolutionary changes among two or more species that are complementary, so that all species benefit
All of the above
questions.expectedAnswer: When two or more species interact, and each acts as an agent of selection causing evolution of the other

questions.heading: apply-it
questions.questionText: What is the difference between positive mutualism and positive commensalism?
options: Positive mutualism occurs when the fitness outcomes for all interacting species are high; positive commensalism occurs when the fitness outcomes some species are high, but others realize no fitness consequences.
Positive mutualism occurs when some interacting species benefit from the relationship and others do not; positive commensalism occurs when only one of the interacting species benefits.
Positive mutualism leads to coevolution of all the interacting species; positive commensalism benefits some species but it does not lead to coevolution.

Natural selection always favors positive mutualism because all interacting species have higher fitness because of the relationship; it does not favor positive commensalism because only one of the interacting species has higher fitness.

Positive mutualism does not lead to coevolution because all the interacting species benefit from the current relationship; positive commensalism leads to coevolution because some of the interacting species benefit, so the others need to evolve to benefit as well. ![img-267.jpeg](img-267.jpeg) ![img-268.jpeg](img-268.jpeg)
questions.expectedAnswer: Positive mutualism occurs when the fitness outcomes for all interacting species are high; positive commensalism occurs when the fitness outcomes some species are high, but others realize no fitness consequences.

Figure 15.18A (right) and 15.18B show that fitness is greatest in both species when bill depth is slightly larger (4mm) than scale thickness, so that birds with shallower or deeper bills than that optimum size will have to specialize on smaller or larger scales, and the populations will diverge.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Müllerian mimicry
questions.expectedAnswer: A theory that proposes that the geographic structure of populations is central to the dynamics of coevolution. questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Reciprocal selection
questions.expectedAnswer: Occurs when species interact antagonistically in a way that results in each species exerting reciprocal directional selection on the other. As one species evolves to overcome the weapons of the other, it, in turn, selects for new weaponry in its opponent.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Define the term: Retrovirus
questions.expectedAnswer: Occurs when several harmful or distasteful species resemble each other in appearance, facilitating the learned avoidance of predators

Group selection favors alleles that contribute to the performance of the group, not necessarily the individual.

Because evolution promotes selfish behavior of individuals, which is not necessarily good for the species.
Because evolution cannot recognize what is best for a group of organisms; it can only act on the best alleles within a population.

Because evolution cannot recognize what is best for a group of organisms; immediate fitness consequences determine the success or failure of an allele, irrespective of the ultimate outcome of the process.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: Which is NOT one of the three conditions necessary for evolution by natural selection to occur?
options: Individuals must differ in the characteristics of a trait.

The differences among individuals in a trait must be at least partially heritable. Incorrect. Heritable variation is integral to natural selection for most organisms because it is an effective mechanism by which genotypic variation may be transmitted. Genotypic variation arises from mutations. Whether they be detrimental, neutral, or beneficial, mutations that affect the phenotype can affect survival and reproduction and therefore be passed to offspring or not (see Chapter 7). Natural selection acts to alter the abundances of those mutations in the population based on the relative reproductive success of individuals (see Section 2.3).
Some individuals survive and reproduce more successfully than others because of differences in a trait.
The more an individual needs a trait, the more quickly it will adapt to its environment.
All are necessary for natural selection to occur.
questions.expectedAnswer: The more an individual needs a trait, the more quickly it will adapt to its environment.

questions.heading: what-and-why
questions.questionText: What would you predict would be the outcome of natural selection on a mutation that increases fertility early in life but increases susceptibility to cancerous growths later in life?
options: Natural selection would favor individuals with the mutation because the fitness effects early in life would be bigger than the harm the mutation causes in old age.

Natural selection would not favor individuals with the mutation because individuals susceptible to cancerous growths would die sooner and have lower lifetime reproductive success than individuals without the mutation.
Natural selection would favor a reaction norm that balanced reproductive success and susceptibility to cancerous growths.
Natural selection would remove individuals with the mutation from the population because mutations are detrimental.
A single mutation cannot be both beneficial and detrimental.
questions.expectedAnswer: Natural selection would favor individuals with the mutation because the fitness effects early in life would be bigger than the harm the mutation causes in old age.
